subject: civic
learning_objectives: List examples of youth empowerment programmes
Explain youth empowerment skills and their importance
Identify problems that affect youth empowerment
Explain the NYSC as a youth empowerment scheme

sections.heading: Meaning of Youth Empowerment
sections.content:
The youth simply means young people. It is a period between childhood and adult age — neither childhood nor adulthood, but somewhere in-between. This group constitutes 47% of the productive population of the country.

Empowerment means increasing the spiritual, political, social, or economic strength of individuals and communities. It is the act of developing confidence in the capabilities of an individual or group.

Youth empowerment is the act of developing skills and leadership qualities in young people that enable them to create solutions, make informed and responsible decisions about their lives. Youth empowerment simply means the act of training youths and giving them the opportunity to do certain things on their own.

sections.heading: Examples of Youth Empowerment Programmes
sections.content:
a. Youth Farmer Club (YFC)
b. African Youth Empowerment Nigeria (AYEN)
c. National Youth Service Corps (NYSC)
d. Boys Scout Movement
e. Citizenship and Leadership Training Centres

sections.heading: Youth Empowerment Skills and Their Importance
sections.content:
a. HIV/AIDS Education Programme: This can be organised for students and young adults. It sensitises them on how to avoid having sex before marriage and how to protect themselves from HIV/AIDS.

b. Abstinence Education Youth Empowerment Skill: This empowers youths with skills. They are taught to develop character traits that will enable them to achieve goals and to build relationships based on mutual respect.

c. Youth Empowerment Studies Workshop: This is a programme that teaches youths how to manage stress and emotions, develop meaningful relationships, and cultivate leadership skills.

d. The Art of Living Programme: This programme is organised for children between 14 and 18 years old. The children are taught how to relax the mind, sharpen the intellect, improve memory, and increase joy. They learn how to deal with negative emotions and stress.

sections.heading: Problems That Affect Youth Empowerment
sections.content:
a. Poor Monitoring: Poor monitoring and supervision of youth programmes are responsible for the poor implementation of most youth empowerment programmes in Nigeria.

b. Poor Management: There is poor management of youth recreational facilities and vocational centres. The facilities are never put in place as funds provided are embezzled.

c. Lack of Funds: When youths are exposed or taught certain jobs or given adequate education, they are expected to put into practice what they have learnt. In most cases, there is no money to do so, and the knowledge received cannot be put to use.

d. Lack of Facilities: The equipment needed for work may not be available. Electricity, motorable roads, and efficient means of transportation and communication may be lacking.

e. Foreign Influence: Nigerian youths are greatly influenced by foreign cultures and values. In places where funds, facilities, and the working environment are conducive, foreign factors can still affect youth empowerment programmes.

f. Social Vices: There are many anti-social behaviours in the country, such as armed robbery, cultism, kidnapping, and get-rich-quick syndrome. All these vices distract youths from achieving set goals.

sections.heading: The National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) as an Empowerment Scheme
sections.content:
The NYSC was established by the military regime of General Yakubu Gowon on 22nd May 1973. The NYSC in Nigeria has helped many Nigerian graduates to be self-employed.

Reasons for the Establishment of the NYSC:
a. To develop common ties among Nigerian youths and promote national unity and integration.
b. To enable Nigerian youths to acquire the spirit of self-reliance by encouraging them to develop skills for self-employment.
c. To remove prejudices, eliminate ignorance, and confirm at first hand the many similarities among Nigerians of all ethnic groups.
d. To inculcate discipline in Nigerian youths by instilling in them a tradition of industry at work and of patriotic and loyal service to Nigeria in any situation they may find themselves.
e. To develop a sense of corporate existence and common destiny of the people of Nigeria.
